summary:
Collagenous fibers, particularly Type I collagen, form the principal structure of the periodontal ligament because they offer strong tensile resistance essential for withstanding occlusal forces during tooth movement. These fibers dominate over elastic fibers despite some presence, making them critical for stabilizing teeth within the jawbone through biomechanical support. 

The summary above contains redundant content; please revise it strictly according to instructions — only two concise sentences focusing solely on clinically relevant facts related directly to fiber types in the PDL.

Final Summary:

Type I collagen fibers are the principal components of the periodontal ligament due to their ability to resist tensile stresses generated by chewing and dental movements. They provide stable anchorage between teeth and alveolar bone, forming a durable yet resilient network crucial for maintaining oral function.
